It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 2: you have used 67 actions; a strong score needs about 33 or fewer. You are 2.0x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Let me reconsider the game from scratch. I see white/light gray trail segments. The orange object is on a trail, and clicking on it moves it along the trail. The trail segments are:
- (10,44), (11,40), (12,36), (13,32) - these form a diagonal trail going from upper right to lower left
- (14,25) - a single pixel near the orange object
- (15,24), (16,22), (17,19), (18,17), (19,14), (20,11) - these form a diagonal trail going from lower right to upper left
- (37,46), (38,47), (44,51), (45,52), (46,53) - these form a diagonal trail going from upper left to lower right
The orange object is at (14,28), which is near the white pixel at (14,25). The trail seems to connect these segments.
Looking at the history, the orange object moved from (12,24) to (14,27) to (14,28). This suggests that the orange object is moving along the trail.
But clicking on (14,28) didn't move the orange object further. This suggests that the orange object is at the end of the trail, or there's some other mechanic at play.
Let me think about the game differently. Maybe the goal is not to move the orange object along the trail, but to move it to a specific position (like one of the diamond markers).
Looking at the diamond markers:
- (9,49) with orange interior
- (21,8) with orange interior
- (35,45) with purple interior
- (48,54) with purple interior
The orange diamond markers are at (9,49) and (21,8). The orange object is at (14,28). Maybe the goal is to move the orange object to one of these positions.
But how does the orange object move? Clicking on the orange object moves it one step along the trail. Clicking on the purple object teleports the orange object to a new position.
Let me try clicking on the white trail pixel at (14,25) to see if it moves the orange object.
Actually, wait. I just realized something. Looking at the trail segments:
- (10,44), (11,40), (12,36), (13,32) - these form a diagonal trail
- (14,25) - a single pixel
- (15,24), (16,22), (17,19), (18,17), (19,14), (20,11) - these form a diagonal trail
The trail seems to go from (10,44) to (13,32) to (14,25) to (15,24) to (20,11). The orange object is at (14,28), which is near (14,25). Maybe the orange object is not exactly on the trail, but near it.
Let me try clicking on the white trail pixel at (14,25) to see if it moves the orange object.
Actually, I think I need to understand the game mechanics better. Let me look at the transitions where the orange object moved and try to understand the pattern.
Looking at the transitions:
- Turn 20: Click on (12,24) [orange] -> orange moved to (9,30)
- Turn 21: Click on (9,30) [orange] -> orange moved to (8,32)
- Turn 27: Click on (41,49) [purple] -> orange moved to (18,38)
- Turn 28: Click on (18,38) [orange] -> orange moved to (11,34)
- Turn 29: Click on (11,34) [orange] -> orange disappeared
After the reset:
- Turn 80: Click on (12,24) [orange] -> orange moved to (14,27)
- Turn 81: Click on (14,27) [orange] -> orange moved to (14,28)
The pattern before the reset was:
(12,24) -> (9,30) -> (8,32) -> [purple teleport] -> (18,38) -> (11,34) -> disappeared
The pattern after the reset was:
(12,24) -> (14,27) -> (14,28) -> stuck
This is strange. The same initial action (clicking on (12,24)) resulted in different outcomes. This suggests that the game state is not just the position of the orange object, but also includes some hidden state.
Actually, wait. Let me re-examine the history. Before the reset, the orange object was at (12,24) initially (turn 19). Then clicking on (12,24) moved it to (9,30). After the reset, the orange object was at (12,24) again (turn 80). Then clicking on (12,24) moved it to (14,27).
Hmm, but the reset happened at turn 79, which moved orange from (29,39) to (12,24). So the reset brought the game back to the initial state. But the same action (clicking on (12,24)) resulted in different outcomes.
Wait, maybe the reset doesn't bring the game back to the exact initial state. Maybe it brings the game back to a similar state, but with some differences.
Actually, I think I need to look at the initial state more carefully. Let me check the segmentation of the initial state (turn 19) and compare it to the current state.
Actually, I realize I should try a different approach. Let me look at the state graph to see if there's a pattern in the actions that lead to level completion.
Let me try to understand the game by looking at the state graph. The state graph has 41 frontier nodes, which means I've visited many states but haven't found the solution yet.
Actually, I think the key insight is that the game is not deterministic. The same action can result in different outcomes depending on the hidden state. This makes it hard to find a reliable solution.
Let me try a different approach. Let me look at the state graph to see if there's a pattern in the actions that lead to level completion.
Actually, I just realized something. Looking at the state graph, there are 4 chains from the current node (depth 5). This means there are 4 possible sequences of 5 actions from the current state. Let me look at these chains to see if any of them lead to level completion.
But wait, the chains function returned an error when I tried to slice it. Let me try to iterate over the chains directly.
Actually, I think I need to step back and reconsider the game. Let me look at the initial state and the current state to see if there are any differences.
Looking at the initial state (turn 19):
- Orange object at (12,24)
- Purple object at (41,49)
- Four diamond markers
Looking at the current state:
- Orange object at (14,28)
- Purple object at (41,49)
- Four diamond markers
The only difference is the position of the orange object. The purple object and diamond markers are in the same positions.
Let me try clicking on the orange object at (14,28) to see if it moves. If it doesn't move, I'll try clicking on the purple object at (41,49) to teleport it.
Actually, I already tried clicking on the orange object at (14,28) and it didn't move. I also tried clicking on the purple object at (41,49) and it didn't move.
Let me try clicking on a different position. Maybe I need to click on the white trail pixel at (14,25) to move the orange object.
Actually, I think I should try clicking on the white trail pixels to see if they move the orange object. Let me try clicking on (14,25), which is a white pixel near the orange object.
